feed.title: Khajan Singh | ISM Portfolio
feed.subtitle: Independent Study and Mentorship (ISM) digital portfolio for Environmental Electrical Engineering.

entry.title: Week #23 - January 19th-January 26th
entry.content:
This week has been one of the busiest and most transformative yet. I’ve been balancing heavy schoolwork alongside preparation for the regional science fair, the upcoming SAT, and BPA State, all happening within the same window. The workload has been intense, but it’s pushed me to manage my time more deliberately than ever before.

The highlight of the week was traveling to Pasadena to visit NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory as part of the Enterprise Neurosystem team. During the meetings, we discussed plans for implementing sensor networks in the Bwindi Impenetrable Forest, focusing on multi‑phase deployment strategies and different sensor topology models to ensure reliability in remote environments. Listening to conversations about large‑scale environmental monitoring systems and seeing how these networks are structured from concept to execution was incredibly inspiring and directly connected to my interests in IoT and distributed sensing systems.

While there, I also had the opportunity to meet my ISM mentor in person, along with numerous professionals, including NASA scientists and experienced technology veterans. Being in that environment made the field feel tangible and reinforced that this is the direction I want to pursue.

Outside of the technical experience, I explored Southern California, mostly around Los Angeles, and even bought my first two vinyl records, a small but memorable milestone. Now that I’m back, the focus shifts to finalizing my science fair board and preparing for the next round of academic challenges.

This week felt like stepping into the future I’m working toward.
entry.summary: This week has been one of the busiest and most transformative yet. I’ve been balancing heavy schoolwork alongside preparation for the regional science fair, the upcoming SAT, and BPA State, all happening within the same window. The workload has been intense, but it’s pushed me to manage my time more deliberately than ever before.

entry.title: Week #22 - January 12th-January 19th
entry.content:
This week, I refined my plant electrophysiology board for the DSRF regional fair. Tweaking the presentation and making sure every detail was clear took time, but I’m proud of how polished it looks.

I’m also thrilled to have been invited to travel to Pasadena for a series of Enterprise Neurosystem conferences, with the possibility of a NASA JPL and Caltech tour. The opportunity to meet with researchers and see cutting‑edge labs energizes me to keep pushing forward.

Despite the excitement, my schedule remains packed: I’m cramming schoolwork, preparing for the March SAT, and carving out time to work out 4–5 days a week. Balancing ambition with discipline is a challenge, but staying consistent with my habits is helping me seize each opportunity without burning out.
entry.summary: This week, I refined my plant electrophysiology board for the DSRF regional fair. Tweaking the presentation and making sure every detail was clear took time, but I’m proud of how polished it looks.

entry.title: Week #21 - January 5th-January 12th
entry.content:
I finally finished assembling the plant electrophysiology board for the science fair. The process of distilling complex experiments into a cohesive presentation taught me a lot about clear communication and has sparked ideas for future research to expand on this work.

I’m getting ready to travel to Pasadena, California, for an Enterprise Neurosystem conference and possibly a tour of NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory and Caltech. The upcoming trip is motivating me to prepare questions and topics to discuss with the professionals I’ll meet there.

At the same time, my schedule feels more packed than ever. I’m studying for USACO competitions, preparing for the March SAT, and keeping up with my regular classes. It’s a challenging balancing act, but I see it as a transition period—moving from project completion toward new research and academic challenges.
entry.summary: I finally finished assembling the plant electrophysiology board for the science fair. The process of distilling complex experiments into a cohesive presentation taught me a lot about clear communication and has sparked ideas for future research to expand on this work.

entry.title: Week #20 - December 29th-January 5th
entry.content:
Our BPA outreach at Freshman Night was a big success. We had so many students sign up that we ran out of posters! Seeing new faces excited about business and technology reinvigorates our club and reminds me why leadership is important.

The snow break also gave me extra time to work on the board for my plant electrophysiology science fair project. Fine‑tuning the visuals and narrative has strengthened my understanding of the data and honed my ability to communicate complex information clearly.

Throughout these experiences, I’m noticing growth in both my leadership and technical abilities. Leading BPA initiatives while delving deeper into engineering projects has helped me identify what kind of engineering work energizes me. Each project brings me closer to understanding my long‑term direction.
entry.summary: Our BPA outreach at Freshman Night was a big success. We had so many students sign up that we ran out of posters! Seeing new faces excited about business and technology reinvigorates our club and reminds me why leadership is important.

entry.title: Week #19 - December 22nd-December 29th
entry.content:
With icy roads closing school for a few days, I took advantage of the unexpected time off to dive deeper into my science fair project. I’m developing an assistive technology for individuals who are auditorily impaired, and the extra hours allowed me to refine my research questions and prototype ideas.

I also continued preparing applications for various summer programs, seeking opportunities that will stretch my understanding of embedded systems, AI, and environmental sensing. As I wait to hear back, I’m staying optimistic and focused on the skills I can develop now.

The Enterprise Neurosystem team is planning a conference at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in California, and there’s a chance I’ll get to attend. The prospect of meeting JPL scientists and discussing our sensor work is incredibly exciting. Meanwhile, the rare Texas snow this week was a fun reminder to appreciate the little moments — a perfect balance of play and productivity.
entry.summary: With icy roads closing school for a few days, I took advantage of the unexpected time off to dive deeper into my science fair project. I’m developing an assistive technology for individuals who are auditorily impaired, and the extra hours allowed me to refine my research questions and prototype ideas.

entry.title: Week #18 - December 15th-December 22nd
entry.content:
Another milestone this week: my BPA team advanced to the state competition! It’s rewarding to see our dedication recognized, and I’m excited to represent our region at the next level. The experience continues to sharpen my teamwork and leadership skills.

In collaboration with the Enterprise Neurosystem, I’ve started implementing sensors for our projects. It’s gratifying to see the research I’ve been doing for ISM translate into real‑world applications, and I’m learning a lot from the process of integrating and calibrating these devices.

I’ve also been applying to several summer programs, each offering unique opportunities for research and exploration in electrical engineering and environmental science. Seeing friends and classmates begin to receive college decisions has made me reflect on my own path; while my journey still has a way to go, I’m motivated by their success to continue building momentum in my projects and applications.
entry.summary: Another milestone this week: my BPA team advanced to the state competition! It’s rewarding to see our dedication recognized, and I’m excited to represent our region at the next level. The experience continues to sharpen my teamwork and leadership skills.

entry.title: Week #17 - December 8th-December 15th
entry.content:
This week brought some exciting milestones. My team qualified for BPA Regionals through our Network Design project and our networking exam score, which was a rewarding payoff for our hard work. We’re now preparing for the next level of competition, and I’m eager to see how far we can go.

In ISM, I started planning and printing a poster board for my plant monitoring project. Organizing the project visually has helped me clarify my narrative and think critically about how to communicate the system’s design and impact. After the long winter break, it took some effort to readjust my study habits, but I’m back into the groove of balancing schoolwork with ISM.

Looking ahead, I’m outlining my final ISM project, which aims to advance the plant monitoring system by integrating AI and new sensor technology. I also want to expand my professional network; after my recent MIT contact, I’m exploring more mentors who can offer fresh perspectives. Building these connections feels like the next step toward turning my ideas into reality.
entry.summary: This week brought some exciting milestones. My team qualified for BPA Regionals through our Network Design project and our networking exam score, which was a rewarding payoff for our hard work. We’re now preparing for the next level of competition, and I’m eager to see how far we can go.

entry.title: Week #16 - December 1st-December 8th
entry.content:
After the chaos of finals, I finally had the chance to reset. I caught up on much needed sleep and used the downtime to reflect on my progress this semester. I also started collaborating on a science fair project with a friend, drafting a research proposal for the MIT THINK Scholars Program. The opportunity to combine our interests into a structured research plan has been both challenging and inspiring.

At the same time, I began researching and applying to summer programs that align with my goals in electrical and environmental engineering. Navigating applications while continuing to study for my BPA test has kept my schedule busy, but it’s exciting to think about the possibilities these opportunities could open up.

Unfortunately, I caught the flu toward the end of the week, which slowed me down more than I anticipated. Even so, I still managed to celebrate Christmas with my family, and the holiday provided a welcome moment of joy and perspective. I’m feeling rested and ready to start the next phase of my journey with renewed resilience.
entry.summary: After the chaos of finals, I finally had the chance to reset. I caught up on much needed sleep and used the downtime to reflect on my progress this semester. I also started collaborating on a science fair project with a friend, drafting a research proposal for the MIT THINK Scholars Program. The opportunity to combine our interests into a structured research plan has been both challenging and inspiring.

entry.title: Week #15 - November 24th-December 1st
entry.content:
This week followed a familiar yet intense cycle of tests and preparation. I finally took the December SAT and, after some reflection, decided to shift my focus to the ACT for upcoming testing. It’s been an exhausting but valuable experience to push through multiple standardized tests, and I’m proud of the resilience I’ve built along the way.

On the mentorship front, I scheduled a call for December 23rd with Dr. Rahul Bhattacharyya from MIT’s Auto‑ID Lab. I’m looking forward to learning about his work on sensing systems and exploring potential research collaboration. Knowing that this conversation is on the calendar has given me an extra boost of motivation as I wrap up the semester.

Academically, I was buried under a mountain of calculus, statistics, and physics tests, all while finalizing my Business Professionals of America competition paper and completing the ISM Original Work deliverables. The workload has been daunting, but it’s also taught me to prioritize effectively and push through to get things done. With the semester coming to a close, I’m determined to complete everything to the best of my ability before the break.
entry.summary: This week followed a familiar yet intense cycle of tests and preparation. I finally took the December SAT and, after some reflection, decided to shift my focus to the ACT for upcoming testing. It’s been an exhausting but valuable experience to push through multiple standardized tests, and I’m proud of the resilience I’ve built along the way.

entry.title: Week #14 - November 17th-November 24th
entry.content:
This week, I finally secured a long‑awaited interview with Leo Hoarty from the Enterprise Neurosystem, which was a significant step forward in my mentorship search. I’m thrilled to have the opportunity to learn from someone with so much experience in environmental sensor networks, and I’m eager to discuss how my skills can contribute to his projects. Preparing for the interview has given me a clearer picture of what I want to gain from a mentor and what I can offer in return.

With Thanksgiving break, I had a few days to rest and catch up on sleep. It was refreshing to take a short pause from the constant demands of school and ISM, and it gave me the chance to return to my personal IoT projects, which I’d been eager to work on. Balancing relaxation with productivity helped me recharge while also making progress on the technologies that excite me.

Even though it was a holiday week, I made sure to keep my academic priorities in sight. I continued to prepare for the upcoming December SAT by reviewing practice questions and strategies. I also drafted a follow‑up email to the MIT professor who expressed interest in my work, ensuring that I maintain momentum in building professional connections. Overall, this week reinforced the importance of balancing rest with purposeful action, keeping me grounded as I take the next steps toward my ISM goals.
entry.summary: This week, I finally secured a long‑awaited interview with Leo Hoarty from the Enterprise Neurosystem, which was a significant step forward in my mentorship search. I’m thrilled to have the opportunity to learn from someone with so much experience in environmental sensor networks, and I’m eager to discuss how my skills can contribute to his projects. Preparing for the interview has given me a clearer picture of what I want to gain from a mentor and what I can offer in return.
